items.title: Regenerate Security Keys and Salts
items.description: Replace default AUTH_KEY, SECURE_AUTH_KEY, LOGGED_IN_KEY, NONCE_KEY and salts with unique values.
items.threat: Cookie forgery - Predictable keys allow session tampering and unauthorized access.
items.verification: All keys are unique and not default/empty

implementation.apache:
# Generate new salts from https://api.wordpress.org/secret-key/1.1/salt/
# Replace in wp-config.php

mitigationSteps: 4. Replace all AUTH_KEY, SECURE_AUTH_KEY, LOGGED_IN_KEY, NONCE_KEY
testing: All cookies invalidated after key change

items.title: Set Secure File and Directory Permissions
items.threat: Information Disclosure - Loose permissions allow reading sensitive files.
items.impact: Database credentials exposed, code injection possible.
testing: Can still read/write WordPress files normally

items.title: Password Protect wp-admin Directory
items.description: Add HTTP Basic Authentication to wp-admin for additional security layer.
items.threat: Brute Force - WordPress login vulnerable to brute force without extra protection.
items.impact: Unauthorized admin access even with weak WordPress passwords.
items.verification: Accessing /wp-admin prompts for HTTP Basic Auth

implementation.apache:
# Create password file
htpasswd -c /etc/apache2/.htpasswd admin

# Add to wp-admin/.htaccess:
AuthType Basic
AuthName "Admin Access"
AuthUserFile /etc/apache2/.htpasswd
Require valid-user
implementation.litespeed:
# Use same .htaccess approach as Apache
# Add authentication directives

mitigationSteps: 5. For Nginx: add to server block
items.rollback: Remove auth directives from .htaccess or nginx config.

items.title: Disable XML-RPC if Not Needed
items.description: XML-RPC is used for brute force amplification and pingback DDoS attacks.
items.threat: Brute Force Amplification - One request can test multiple passwords via XML-RPC.
items.impact: Account compromise, participation in DDoS attacks.

implementation.apache:
# Add to .htaccess:
<Files xmlrpc.php>
Require all denied
</Files>
implementation.litespeed:
# Add to .htaccess:
<Files xmlrpc.php>
Require all denied
</Files>

mitigationSteps: 1. Verify XML-RPC is not needed (Jetpack, mobile apps, pingbacks)
2. Add blocking rules to server config
testing: WordPress mobile apps fail to connect (if disabled)
items.rollback: Remove blocking rules or uninstall plugin.

items.title: Restrict REST API Access
items.description: Limit REST API endpoints to authenticated users to prevent user enumeration.
items.impact: Attackers get valid usernames for brute force attacks.
items.verification: API requires authentication for user data

implementation.litespeed:
# Same approach as Apache
# Add code to functions.php or use plugin

mitigationSteps: 4. Verify logged-in users can still access API

items.title: Change Default Database Table Prefix
items.description: Change wp_ to random prefix to slow down SQL injection attacks.
items.threat: SQL Injection - Default table names make attacks easier.

implementation.apache:
# Method 1: During installation
# Set custom prefix in setup

# Method 2: For existing site
# Edit wp-config.php: $table_prefix = 'xyz123_';
# Use plugin or manual SQL to rename tables

items.rollback: Restore database backup and revert wp-config.php.

items.title: Disable WP_DEBUG and Error Display
items.description: Debug mode exposes paths, database names, and stack traces.
items.threat: Information Disclosure - Debug output reveals sensitive system information.
items.impact: Attackers learn file paths, database structure, vulnerable code locations.
items.verification: No PHP errors displayed on frontend
testing: Visit site with trigger error - no display
View page source - no error messages
PHP errors logged to file only
items.rollback: Set WP_DEBUG back to true for troubleshooting.

items.title: Hide WordPress Version Number
items.description: Remove WordPress version from meta tags, feeds, and scripts.
items.threat: Version Disclosure - Attackers target specific vulnerable versions.
items.verification: No WordPress version in page source
testing: View page source - no meta generator tag
Check readme.html - removed or access denied
items.rollback: Remove the filter code from functions.php.

items.title: Disable Theme and Plugin Editor
items.description: Prevent editing of theme and plugin files from admin panel.
items.threat: Code Injection - Compromised admin can inject malicious code directly.
items.impact: Backdoor installation, malware injection, site defacement.
items.verification: No 'Editor' menu under Appearance or Plugins
mitigationSteps: 4. Login to admin and verify Editor is gone
5. Use SFTP for file edits instead
testing: Direct URL to editor returns error

items.title: Enable Automatic Security Updates
items.description: Enable auto-updates for WordPress core security releases and minor versions.
items.threat: Known Vulnerabilities - Delayed patching leaves site exploitable.
items.impact: Site compromise through known, patched vulnerabilities.
mitigationSteps:
1. Choose update strategy:
   - true = all updates
   - 'minor' = security only (recommended)

items.title: Force SSL on Admin Pages
items.description: Force HTTPS for wp-admin and login pages.
testing: Access /wp-admin via HTTP - redirects to HTTPS

testing: Same IP blocked for configured time

items.title: Configure Regular Automated Backups
items.description: Schedule automated backups of database and files to secure location.
items.threat: Data Loss - Without backups, site loss is permanent.
items.impact: Permanent loss of content, configuration, business data.

implementation.apache:
# Install: UpdraftPlus or BackupBuddy
# Configure daily backups to cloud storage

mitigationSteps: 4. Set remote storage (Google Drive, S3, etc.)

items.title: Add Security Headers
items.description: Implement HTTP security headers: CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, X-XSS-Protection.
items.threat: XSS, Clickjacking, MitM - Missing headers allow various attacks.
items.impact: Cross-site scripting, clickjacking, protocol downgrade attacks.
items.verification: Security headers present in HTTP responses

implementation.apache:
# Add to .htaccess:
Header always set X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN"
Header always set X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block"
Header always set X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"
Header always set Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
Header always set Content-Security-Policy "default-src 'self'"

mitigationSteps: 2. Add headers to server config or .htaccess
testing: scan at securityheaders.com shows A or A+

items.title: Disable WP-Cron and Use System Cron
items.description: Replace WordPress pseudo-cron with real system cron for reliability and performance.
items.threat: Performance - WP-Cron runs on page load, slowing site.
items.impact: Slow page loads, missed scheduled tasks, high server load.

implementation.apache:
# In wp-config.php:
define('DISABLE_WP_CRON', true);

# Add to crontab:
*/5 * * * * www-data /usr/bin/php /var/www/html/wp-cron.php > /dev/null 2>&1

testing: No wp-cron requests in page loads
Cron logs show execution every 5 min
items.rollback: Remove DISABLE_WP_CRON and delete cron job.
